edges.description: Malignant Hyperthermia is inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern.
Malignant Hyperthermia is classified as a pharmacogenetic disorder, meaning it's a genetic condition influenced by drugs.
Malignant Hyperthermia primarily affects skeletal muscle tissue.
Halogenated inhalational anesthetics can trigger Malignant Hyperthermia in susceptible individuals.
Succinylcholine is a muscle relaxant that can also act as a trigger for Malignant Hyperthermia.
Malignant Hyperthermia is characterized by a defect in the Ryanodine Receptor (RYR1).
Mutations in the RYR1 gene lead to abnormal Calcium-Induced Calcium Release (CICR) in skeletal muscle cells.
Mutations in the CACNA1S gene can also lead to abnormal Calcium-Induced Calcium Release (CICR).
Abnormal Calcium-Induced Calcium Release (CICR) directly results in uncontrolled release of intracellular calcium.
The defect in the Ryanodine Receptor (RYR1) is the underlying cause for uncontrolled calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
The sarcoplasmic reticulum is the primary source from which uncontrolled intracellular calcium is released.
Uncontrolled calcium release floods calcium ions into the myoplasm, the cytoplasm of muscle cells.
Uncontrolled calcium release leads to a massive surge in intracellular calcium concentrations.
The surge in intracellular calcium directly causes sustained muscle contraction.
Sustained muscle contraction manifests clinically as rigidity, including masseter spasm or generalized rigidity.
Excess intracellular calcium activates numerous biochemical processes, leading to a state of hypermetabolism.
The hypermetabolic state leads to massive depletion of ATP, the cell's energy currency.
Massive ATP depletion causes cell membrane damage, which results in rhabdomyolysis.
Hypermetabolism involves the activation of myosin-actin cross-bridging, a key process in muscle contraction.
The hypermetabolic state also involves the activation of ATP-consuming SERCA pumps, which attempt to resequester calcium.
Activated myosin-actin cross-bridging is the molecular basis for sustained muscle contraction.
SERCA pumps actively transport intracellular calcium ions back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum, consuming ATP in the process.
The hypermetabolic state in MH significantly increases the body's oxygen consumption.
Hypermetabolism results in a massive increase in carbon dioxide production.
Intense biochemical activity during hypermetabolism generates a large amount of heat.
Increased heat production is the direct cause of hyperthermia, a rapid rise in body temperature.
Massive carbon dioxide production leads to hypercarbia, an elevated level of CO2 in the blood.
The metabolic crisis of hypermetabolism results in severe metabolic acidosis due to increased lactic acid and other metabolites.
Increased CO2 production, if not adequately cleared, contributes to respiratory acidosis.
Metabolic acidosis contributes to a generalized state of acidemia in the blood.
Respiratory acidosis also contributes to a generalized state of acidemia in the blood.
Severe ATP depletion critically compromises the integrity of cell membranes, leading to their damage.
Damage to muscle cell membranes is the hallmark of rhabdomyolysis.
Rhabdomyolysis causes the destruction of muscle cells, leading to the leakage of their intracellular contents into the bloodstream.
Potassium is an intracellular content that leaks into the bloodstream during rhabdomyolysis.
Creatine kinase, an enzyme abundant in muscle cells, leaks into the bloodstream during rhabdomyolysis.
Myoglobin, a muscle protein, leaks into the bloodstream during rhabdomyolysis and can cause kidney damage.
The leakage of potassium from damaged muscle cells into the bloodstream causes hyperkalemia.
The massive metabolic demand created by hypermetabolism is an early cause of tachycardia.
Acidemia, a state of blood acidity, is another factor that contributes to tachycardia.
Acidemia contributes to clinical manifestations such as hypercarbia, often worsening the respiratory acidosis.
Dantrolene, the antidote for MH, directly acts on the Ryanodine Receptor (RYR1).
By acting on RYR1, Dantrolene stops the uncontrolled release of calcium, thereby reversing the pathological process.
Uncontrolled calcium release is the central pathophysiological event leading to the full syndrome of Malignant Hyperthermia.
